critique.summary: The article compares data manipulation capabilities in Clojure's tablecloth library with those in R's tidyverse (dplyr), Python's pandas, and polars, using the Palmer Penguin dataset as an example. It provides side-by-side code snippets for common data science tasks, highlighting differences in syntax, paradigms, and handling of missing data.
mainPoints: Tablecloth in Clojure emphasizes functional programming and immutability, contrasting with pandas' mutable data structures.

Basic data operations like reading data, selecting columns, filtering rows, and sorting are similar across tablecloth, dplyr, pandas, and polars, with minor differences in syntax and missing value handling.
Tablecloth requires explicit handling of missing values due to Clojure's use of nil and strong typing, unlike other libraries that have built-in missing value semantics.
Advanced operations such as reshaping data, creating new columns, renaming columns, and grouping/aggregating are demonstrated with equivalent code in all four libraries.
Grouping in tablecloth returns a new dataset with grouped data, differing from pandas and polars which return intermediate objects.
The article serves as both a translation guide and an introduction to the differing programming philosophies behind these data science tools.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately presents code examples and comparisons between Clojure's tablecloth, R's tidyverse (dplyr), Python's pandas, and polars libraries. The descriptions of functional programming concepts, immutability in Clojure, and handling of missing data are consistent with known characteristics of these languages and libraries. The Palmer Penguin dataset is commonly used for such demonstrations, and the code snippets align with documented usage of the respective libraries. The article does not make unsupported claims and provides version information for reproducibility.
considerations: Performance differences between these libraries and languages are not deeply analyzed, which can be significant in real-world applications.
The learning curve and ecosystem maturity for each tool, especially for newcomers to functional programming in Clojure, are not discussed.
The article assumes familiarity with installation and basic usage of the libraries, which may limit accessibility for beginners.
Potential differences in community support, extensibility, and integration with other data science tools are not covered.
The article does not address how these tools handle very large datasets or distributed computing scenarios.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the suitability of Clojure for data manipulation compared to languages like R and Python, with some commenters advocating for typed languages like D and Rust for safety and reliability. Others reflect on Clojure's strengths and challenges in adoption within the data science community, while some highlight syntax preferences and ecosystem considerations.

comments.author: teleforce
comments.text:
All the comparisons are with scripting and untyped languages perhaps for faster development and more intuitive eco-system to increase developer productivity.

In the age of IntelliSense, auto-completion and AI assisted coding, does the choice of scripting and untyped language justifiable for increased in productivity at the expense of safety and reliability?

If you're building data system not just for exploratory, surely modern compiled and typed system languages like Rust and D language make more sense for safety and reliability for the end users?

Even more so with D language where you can even have scripting capability for exploratory and protyping stage with its built-in REPL facility [1],[2]. This is feasible due to its very fast compile time unlike Rust. It has more intuitive "Phytonic" syntax compared to other typed languages [3]. You can also program with GC on by default if you choose to. Apparently, you can have your cake and eat it too.

[1] drepl:

https://github.com/dlang-community/drepl

[2] Why I use the D programming language for scripting:

https://opensource.com/article/21/1/d-scripting

[3] All in on DLang: Why I pivoted to D for web, teaching, and graphics in 2025 and beyond! [PDF]

https://dconf.org/2025/slides/shah.pdf
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced perspective on the trade-offs between scripting/untyped languages and compiled/typed languages, emphasizing safety and reliability, supported by references to D and Rust.

comments.author: ertucetin
comments.text:
I’ve built many different kinds of software (backend, frontend, 3D games, cli tools, code editor, and more) with Clojure and have been using it for over a decade now.

I can confidently say that, among the list I mentioned, it’s the best for data manipulation/transformation. Thanks to the author for presenting it clearly and showing how the libraries and code look across different languages, all of which do a great job.

But Clojure has its own special place (maybe in my heart as well :). I think Clojure should be used more in the data science space. Thanks to the JVM, it can be very performant (I’m looking at you, Python).
comments.analysis: The author shares personal experience and a positive endorsement of Clojure's capabilities in data manipulation, providing a credible viewpoint from long-term usage.

comments.author: soumyaskartha
comments.text: Clojure never got the data science crowd even though the language is genuinely good for it. Always felt like a distribution problem more than a technical one.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully addresses the adoption challenges of Clojure in data science, attributing it more to distribution and ecosystem issues than technical limitations.

comments.author: zmmmmm
comments.text:
Seems like it's going to be a tough sell to get people to want to write

    (tc/select-rows ds #(> (% "year") 2008))
instead of

    filter(ds, year > 2008)
They seem to ignore the existance of Spark, so even if you specifically want to use JVM it feels clearer and simpler:

    ds.filter(r => r.year > 2008)
comments.analysis: Highlights usability and syntax clarity concerns when adopting certain Clojure data manipulation idioms, also noting the omission of Spark in comparisons, which is relevant for JVM users.

comments.author: QubridAI
comments.text: Interesting perspective Clojure’s immutable, functional approach makes data wrangling feel very different from the more imperative style of R and Python.
comments.analysis: Provides a concise observation on the fundamental paradigm differences between Clojure and more traditional data science languages, which is accurate and relevant.

comments.author: __mharrison__
comments.text: Good pandas and polars code should also be written in an immutable way...
comments.analysis: Suggests best practices in Python data libraries emphasizing immutability, aligning with modern functional programming principles.

comments.author: olivia-banks
comments.text: Having "NA" being treated as nil/null/None by default seems like it would cause the Namibia problem!
comments.analysis: Raises a humorous but valid concern about ambiguous handling of missing data values, which can cause confusion or errors.

comments.author: thrawa8387336
comments.text: I always wished Incanter took off.
comments.analysis: Expresses a personal wish without adding substantive analysis or new information to the discussion.
